stem: A 28-year-old woman in her second pregnancy is reviewed at 20 weeks gestation. Her first pregnancy ended in a spontaneous second-trimester loss at 18 weeks with painless cervical dilatation. Her current pregnancy has been uncomplicated so far. A transvaginal ultrasound at her morphology scan shows a cervical length of 18 mm with funnelling of the internal os. She has no contractions, no vaginal bleeding, and the membranes are intact. The foetus has normal anatomy on ultrasound.

data_table.Investigations: Cervical length 18 mm with funnelling; foetal anatomy normal; amniotic fluid index normal; group B streptococcus status unknown

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly as monotherapy
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is effective for preventing preterm birth in women with a short cervix found incidentally on ultrasound without a history of cervical insufficiency. However, this woman has a classical history of cervical insufficiency (previous painless second-trimester loss) with a short cervix and funnelling. She is a candidate for cerclage, which provides superior outcomes in this specific high-risk population. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Bed rest and weekly cervical length surveillance by ultrasound
options.rationale: Bed rest has not been shown to prevent preterm birth or cervical shortening in any randomised trial and is no longer recommended. Serial cervical length monitoring alone does not treat the underlying cervical insufficiency. Active intervention is warranted given the history and ultrasound findings. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Commence tocolytic therapy with nifedipine to prevent contractions
options.rationale: Tocolytic therapy is used for acute preterm labour with contractions to buy time for corticosteroid administration. This woman has no contractions - her cervical changes are due to cervical insufficiency, not preterm labour. Tocolytics do not treat cervical incompetence and would not prevent further dilatation. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm Labour)

options.text: Insert a cervical cerclage and commence vaginal progesterone
options.rationale: Cervical cerclage is the correct intervention for cervical insufficiency with a compatible history and short cervix. However, in current practice for history-indicated cerclage, the optimal timing is 12-14 weeks gestation. At 20 weeks with a cervix of 18 mm and funnelling, this is now a rescue cerclage, and adding vaginal progesterone to cerclage has not been shown to provide additional benefit in women already receiving cerclage. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Insert an emergency rescue cerclage after excluding infection
options.rationale: This woman has a classic history of cervical insufficiency (previous painless mid-trimester loss) and now has a short cervix at 18 mm with funnelling at 20 weeks. This presentation warrants rescue cerclage. Before insertion, subclinical infection must be excluded with amniocentesis or vaginal swabs, as cerclage in the presence of infection increases the risk of complications. The window for cerclage typically closes at 24 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency is characterised by painless cervical dilatation leading to second-trimester pregnancy loss. Management depends on the clinical scenario. History-indicated cerclage is placed at 12-14 weeks in women with a classic history. Ultrasound-indicated cerclage is placed when a short cervix (typically <25 mm) is detected in a woman with a prior preterm birth or mid-trimester loss. Rescue cerclage is performed when cervical changes are advanced (dilatation with or without membrane prolapse). Infection must be excluded before rescue cerclage.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage is ideally placed at 12-14 weeks gestation
Rescue cerclage at 20 weeks requires exclusion of subclinical infection before insertion
Vaginal progesterone is first-line for incidental short cervix without a history of cervical insufficiency
Bed rest has no proven benefit for cervical insufficiency or preterm birth prevention
The window for cerclage generally closes at 24 weeks gestation
explanation.pearls: The distinction between history-indicated (12-14 weeks), ultrasound-indicated (<25 mm cervix in high-risk woman), and rescue cerclage (advanced changes with membranes visible) determines both timing and pre-operative requirements - rescue cerclage mandates amniocentesis or vaginal cultures to exclude infection.

stem: A 34-year-old multiparous woman at 39 weeks gestation is in active labour with artificial rupture of membranes performed 20 minutes ago. She suddenly becomes agitated and dyspnoeic, then loses consciousness. Within minutes she develops tonic-clonic seizure activity. After initial resuscitation and seizure termination with midazolam, she is intubated. An emergency caesarean section is performed. Post-delivery, she develops profuse uterine bleeding that does not respond to uterotonics. Blood is noted to be non-clotting from IV sites and surgical wound.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 135, BP 72/40 mmHg, SpO2 88% on FiO2 1.0, Temp 36.2
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.08, lactate 8.4 mmol/L; FBC: Hb 68 g/L, platelets 32 x10^9/L; coags: PT 28 sec, APTT 62 sec, fibrinogen 0.4 g/L; D-dimer >20,000 ng/mL

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and give cryoprecipitate
options.rationale: This presentation is consistent with amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) complicated by disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) and haemorrhagic shock. The immediate priority is addressing the coagulopathy and haemorrhage through massive transfusion protocol activation. Cryoprecipitate is critical to replace fibrinogen (target >2 g/L in obstetric haemorrhage). Packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma, and platelets should be given concurrently in a ratio-based approach. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH; NBA: Massive Transfusion)

options.text: Administer IV heparin infusion to treat the underlying DIC
options.rationale: Heparin is sometimes used in chronic compensated DIC but is contraindicated in acute consumptive DIC with active haemorrhage. Giving heparin to a patient with fibrinogen of 0.4 g/L and profuse bleeding would worsen the haemorrhage catastrophically. The treatment for acute DIC with bleeding is replacement of consumed clotting factors. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulfate for presumed eclamptic seizures
options.rationale: Eclampsia should be considered in any pregnant woman with seizures. However, the clinical picture here - sudden cardiovascular collapse with hypoxia immediately after membrane rupture, followed by DIC with non-clotting blood - is characteristic of AFE, not eclampsia. Eclampsia does not typically cause DIC or refractory haemorrhage. Treatment of the coagulopathy is the priority. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Perform perimortem hysterectomy to control uterine haemorrhage
options.rationale: Hysterectomy may ultimately be necessary if haemorrhage cannot be controlled, but it should not be the first intervention. The coagulopathy must be corrected first - operating on a patient with fibrinogen 0.4 g/L and platelets of 32 will result in uncontrollable surgical bleeding. Massive transfusion and coagulation factor replacement take priority. (Source: NBA: Massive Transfusion)

options.text: Administer IV tranexamic acid 1 g and repeat uterotonics only
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an important adjunct in obstetric haemorrhage and should be given. However, uterotonics have already failed and the fundamental problem is DIC with consumed clotting factors. Tranexamic acid alone cannot replace fibrinogen or clotting factors. The priority is massive transfusion protocol with cryoprecipitate to restore haemostasis. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency with a mortality rate of 20-60%. It classically presents with sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, and seizures during labour or immediately postpartum, followed by DIC and haemorrhage. The pathophysiology involves an abnormal maternal immune response to foetal material entering the circulation. Management is supportive - there is no specific treatment. The immediate priority is haemodynamic resuscitation and correction of coagulopathy through massive transfusion.
key_points: AFE classically presents with sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, seizures, and DIC
DIC in AFE is consumptive - treat with replacement of clotting factors, not heparin
Fibrinogen target in obstetric haemorrhage is above 2 g/L - cryoprecipitate is the fastest way to replace it
Massive transfusion protocol should be activated early with ratio-based product administration
Hysterectomy may be needed but only after coagulopathy correction - surgical haemostasis requires functional clotting
explanation.pearls: In obstetric DIC from AFE, fibrinogen falls earlier and more precipitously than in non-obstetric DIC because baseline fibrinogen in pregnancy is already elevated (4-6 g/L) - a level that appears 'normal' at 2 g/L actually represents a 50% drop and warrants urgent cryoprecipitate.

stem: A 35-year-old woman in her fourth pregnancy is found to have an anterior low-lying placenta at her 20-week morphology scan. She has had two previous caesarean sections and one vaginal delivery. She is asymptomatic with no vaginal bleeding. The sonographer notes lacunae within the placental substance and loss of the normal retroplacental clear zone on ultrasound. The woman asks about the implications of these findings.

data_table.Investigations: Morphology scan: anterior low-lying placenta covering internal os, multiple placental lacunae, loss of retroplacental clear zone, bladder wall irregularity

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Reassure that the placenta will likely migrate and rescan at 32 weeks
options.rationale: While placental migration does occur (up to 90% of low-lying placentas at 20 weeks will resolve), the ultrasound features described - lacunae, loss of retroplacental clear zone, and bladder wall irregularity - are highly suggestive of placenta accreta spectrum. This changes the management from expectant to planned multidisciplinary delivery. Reassurance alone is inappropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Refer to a tertiary centre for MRI and multidisciplinary planning
options.rationale: The combination of previous caesarean sections, anterior placenta praevia, and ultrasound features of accreta (lacunae, loss of clear zone, bladder involvement) constitutes suspected placenta accreta spectrum. Management requires referral to a tertiary centre with interventional radiology, urology, and experienced obstetric surgical teams. MRI can further characterise the depth of invasion. Planned delivery is typically at 35-36 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Schedule elective caesarean section at 39 weeks at the local hospital
options.rationale: Waiting until 39 weeks risks emergency presentation with life-threatening haemorrhage in suspected placenta accreta spectrum. Guidelines recommend planned delivery at 35-36 weeks at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary surgical capability. Delivery at a local hospital without appropriate resources risks maternal death from uncontrollable haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Commence low-dose aspirin to prevent placental insufficiency
options.rationale: Low-dose aspirin is used for pre-eclampsia prevention in high-risk women but has no role in managing placenta accreta spectrum. The abnormal placentation is already established by 20 weeks and aspirin cannot alter the depth of placental invasion. Aspirin may also theoretically increase bleeding risk in a condition already associated with massive haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Arrange serial ultrasounds every 2 weeks to monitor invasion depth
options.rationale: Serial ultrasound monitoring is part of the follow-up plan but is not the most appropriate management in isolation. The finding of suspected placenta accreta spectrum in a woman with multiple prior caesareans requires immediate referral to a tertiary centre for comprehensive planning, not just surveillance at the local facility. The invasion depth does not change the need for tertiary referral. (Source: FIGO: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum describes abnormal placental adherence to the myometrium, ranging from accreta (superficial) through increta (into myometrium) to percreta (through myometrium to adjacent organs). The major risk factor is placenta praevia overlying a previous caesarean scar. Ultrasound features include placental lacunae, loss of the retroplacental clear zone, and bladder wall involvement. Management requires multidisciplinary planning at a tertiary centre with planned caesarean hysterectomy typically at 35-36 weeks.
key_points: Placenta praevia overlying previous caesarean scar significantly increases accreta risk (up to 60% with 3 prior sections)
Ultrasound features: lacunae, loss of retroplacental clear zone, bladder wall irregularity, bridging vessels
MRI adds value for assessing depth of myometrial invasion and parametrial involvement
Delivery is typically planned at 35-36 weeks at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team
Caesarean hysterectomy is often required - attempting placental removal risks life-threatening haemorrhage
explanation.pearls: The risk of placenta accreta spectrum increases exponentially with the number of prior caesarean sections - 3% after one, 11% after two, 40% after three, and 67% after four or more when combined with placenta praevia.

stem: A 3-year-old girl presents with a 5-day history of bloody diarrhoea followed by 2 days of decreasing urine output and pallor. She attended a petting zoo one week before symptom onset. On examination she is pale with periorbital oedema. She has not received any antibiotics during this illness. Stool culture has returned positive for Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli O157:H7.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, BP 108/72 mmHg (above 95th centile), RR 28, Temp 37.1
data_table.Investigations: FBC: Hb 72 g/L, WCC 14.8 x10^9/L, platelets 38 x10^9/L; blood film: schistocytes present, helmet cells; UEC: creatinine 280 micromol/L (baseline ~30), urea 22 mmol/L, K+ 5.9 mmol/L, Na+ 132 mmol/L; LDH 2,400 U/L; haptoglobin <0.1 g/L; reticulocyte count elevated

lead_in: Which of the following interventions should be specifically avoided?

options.text: Platelet transfusion to correct the severe thrombocytopenia
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is relatively contraindicated in typical HUS as it may fuel the thrombotic microangiopathy by providing substrate for further microvascular thrombosis. However, it may be given for active life-threatening bleeding or before invasive procedures. While it should be avoided if possible, it is not the most specifically contraindicated intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: HUS)

options.text: Packed red cell transfusion to maintain haemoglobin above 70 g/L
options.rationale: Red cell transfusion is appropriate and recommended in HUS when haemoglobin falls below 60-70 g/L or the patient is symptomatic. Unlike platelet transfusion, packed red cells do not contribute to the thrombotic microangiopathy. Transfusion should be given slowly to avoid fluid overload in the setting of renal failure. (Source: RCH CPG: HUS)

options.text: IV fluid resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid for rehydration
options.rationale: Careful IV fluid management is essential in HUS. Early volume expansion may reduce the severity of renal injury. However, fluid must be carefully titrated in the setting of oliguria and hypertension to avoid fluid overload. Isotonic crystalloid is appropriate for initial resuscitation. (Source: RCH CPG: HUS)

options.text: Antibiotic therapy targeting the Shiga toxin-producing E. coli
options.rationale: Antibiotics are specifically contraindicated in Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) infection complicated by or at risk of HUS. Antibiotic-mediated bacterial lysis releases additional Shiga toxin, which may worsen the thrombotic microangiopathy and increase the risk of progression to HUS or worsening of established HUS. This is one of the few infections where antibiotics are actively harmful. (Source: RCH CPG: HUS)

options.text: Renal replacement therapy if oliguria persists or K+ rises
options.rationale: Renal replacement therapy (dialysis) is indicated in HUS for severe or refractory AKI with complications such as refractory hyperkalaemia, fluid overload, or uraemic encephalopathy. It is an important part of supportive management and should not be avoided. Approximately 50-70% of children with STEC-HUS require dialysis during the acute phase. (Source: RCH CPG: HUS)

explanation.summary: Typical (STEC-associated) haemolytic uraemic syndrome is the most common cause of acute kidney injury in young children. It follows diarrhoeal illness caused by Shiga toxin-producing E. coli, most commonly O157:H7. The triad is microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury. Antibiotics are specifically contraindicated as they increase Shiga toxin release and may worsen the disease. Management is supportive with careful fluid balance, blood transfusion as needed, and dialysis for severe AKI.
key_points: Antibiotics are contraindicated in STEC infection - they increase Shiga toxin release and may worsen HUS
The classic triad is microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and AKI
Schistocytes on blood film confirm microangiopathic haemolysis
Platelet transfusion is relatively contraindicated as it may fuel the thrombotic microangiopathy
50-70% of children with STEC-HUS require dialysis during the acute phase
explanation.pearls: Antibiotic administration during STEC infection increases the risk of developing HUS by 14-17 fold - the SOS response triggered by antibiotics (especially fluoroquinolones and trimethoprim) upregulates Shiga toxin production and release from dying bacteria.

stem: A 5-week-old first-born male infant presents with a 10-day history of progressively forceful, non-bilious vomiting that now occurs after every feed. His mother describes the vomit as projectile. He is hungry immediately after vomiting. He has lost 200 g from his 2-week weight check. On examination he is mildly dehydrated with a sunken fontanelle. A firm olive-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant during a test feed. Ultrasound confirms pyloric stenosis with a pyloric muscle thickness of 5 mm and channel length of 18 mm.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160, RR 42, SpO2 99% RA, Temp 36.8, Weight 3.6 kg
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.52, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 38 mmol/L, K+ 2.8 mmol/L, Na+ 133 mmol/L, Cl- 82 mmol/L (ref 98-106)

lead_in: What must be corrected before surgical intervention?

options.text: The metabolic acidosis, to reduce anaesthetic cardiac risk
options.rationale: This infant has a metabolic alkalosis (pH 7.52, HCO3 38), not acidosis. The elevated pCO2 represents respiratory compensation (hypoventilation to retain CO2). Pyloric stenosis causes loss of gastric hydrochloric acid through vomiting, producing hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Identifying the correct acid-base disturbance is essential for pre-operative correction. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: The thrombocytopenia, to reduce the risk of surgical bleeding
options.rationale: The blood results do not show thrombocytopenia. This is a distractor - pyloric stenosis does not cause thrombocytopenia. The key pre-operative concern is the metabolic alkalosis with hypokalaemia and hypochloraemia, which must be corrected with IV 0.9% NaCl with KCl supplementation before safe anaesthesia. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: The hypernatraemia, to prevent intraoperative seizures
options.rationale: The sodium is 133 mmol/L, which is low-normal to mildly hyponatraemic, not hypernatraemic. Pyloric stenosis typically causes hyponatraemia from volume depletion and aldosterone-mediated sodium retention being overwhelmed by losses. The key correction needed is the metabolic alkalosis, hypochloraemia, and hypokalaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: The hypoglycaemia, to provide adequate energy for healing
options.rationale: While infants with pyloric stenosis can develop hypoglycaemia from poor oral intake, the investigations provided do not include a BSL result indicating hypoglycaemia. The critical pre-operative correction in pyloric stenosis is the electrolyte and acid-base disturbance - specifically the hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: The hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with IV fluids
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis causes loss of gastric HCl, producing hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. This must be corrected before anaesthesia because alkalosis shifts the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve leftward (impairing tissue oxygen delivery), hypokalaemia predisposes to cardiac arrhythmias under anaesthesia, and compensatory hypoventilation may cause apnoea during induction. Correction is achieved with IV 0.9% NaCl plus KCl supplementation over 24-48 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

explanation.summary: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis classically presents in first-born males at 3-6 weeks with progressive projectile non-bilious vomiting. The pathognomonic metabolic derangement is hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from gastric acid loss. Pyloromyotomy is curative but is never an emergency - the metabolic abnormalities must be corrected first. IV 0.9% NaCl with potassium supplementation is the resuscitation fluid of choice. Surgery should only proceed once chloride exceeds 100 mmol/L and bicarbonate is below 26 mmol/L.
key_points: Pyloric stenosis causes hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from HCl loss in vomit
Pyloromyotomy is not an emergency - correct the metabolic derangement before surgery
IV 0.9% NaCl with KCl is the resuscitation fluid - replaces chloride and potassium
Surgical readiness targets: chloride >100 mmol/L, bicarbonate <26 mmol/L, potassium >3.5 mmol/L
Respiratory compensation for metabolic alkalosis is hypoventilation - which risks apnoea under anaesthesia
explanation.pearls: The kidneys initially compensate for the alkalosis by excreting bicarbonate in urine (alkaline urine), but as volume depletion worsens, the kidneys prioritise sodium reabsorption over acid-base correction, producing the paradoxical aciduria of pyloric stenosis.

stem: A 52-year-old man is being reviewed in the outpatient clinic 3 months after a segmental pulmonary embolism. He has completed 3 months of rivaroxaban. The PE was unprovoked with no identifiable transient risk factor. He has had no recurrent symptoms. Age-appropriate cancer screening including CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis was negative. His estimated bleeding risk is low. He asks when he can stop anticoagulation.

data_table.Investigations: D-dimer (off anticoagulation for 2 weeks): elevated at 0.8 mg/L FEU; residual thrombus on CTPA follow-up

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation recommendation?

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban 20 mg daily indefinitely with annual review
options.rationale: For a first unprovoked PE with low bleeding risk, Australian and international guidelines recommend extended (indefinite) anticoagulation. The recurrence rate after stopping anticoagulation for unprovoked VTE is approximately 10% at 1 year and 30% at 5 years. The elevated D-dimer off anticoagulation and residual thrombus further support continuing therapy. Annual bleeding risk reassessment is recommended. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Cease anticoagulation now as the 3-month course is sufficient
options.rationale: Three months is the minimum treatment duration for unprovoked VTE, not the recommended total duration. Stopping at 3 months for unprovoked PE carries a high recurrence risk (approximately 10% at 1 year). Extended anticoagulation is recommended when bleeding risk is low, particularly with an elevated D-dimer and residual thrombus. (Source: CHEST VTE Guidelines)

options.text: Switch to aspirin 100 mg daily as extended secondary prevention
options.rationale: The ASPIRE and WARFASA trials showed aspirin provides a modest 30% relative risk reduction in VTE recurrence compared to placebo. However, this is significantly inferior to continued anticoagulation, which provides a 80-90% relative risk reduction. Aspirin is only considered when anticoagulation is contraindicated or refused. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Stop rivaroxaban and repeat D-dimer in 4 weeks to guide decision
options.rationale: The D-dimer has already been checked off anticoagulation and is elevated, which predicts higher recurrence risk. The decision framework is already clear: unprovoked PE, low bleeding risk, elevated D-dimer, residual thrombus - all favour extended anticoagulation. Further D-dimer testing would not change the recommendation. (Source: CHEST VTE Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce to rivaroxaban 10 mg daily for 6 months then reassess
options.rationale: Reduced-dose rivaroxaban (10 mg daily) has been shown in the EINSTEIN CHOICE trial to be effective for extended VTE prevention with lower bleeding risk than the full 20 mg dose. This is a reasonable long-term strategy after an initial treatment period at full dose, but the question asks about the overall recommendation. The key message is that anticoagulation should continue indefinitely, not stop at a fixed point. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: The duration of anticoagulation for VTE is determined by whether the event was provoked or unprovoked and the patient's bleeding risk. Provoked VTE with a clear transient risk factor (surgery, immobilisation) is treated for 3 months. Unprovoked VTE carries a high recurrence risk and warrants extended anticoagulation when bleeding risk is low. Factors favouring extended therapy include male sex, elevated D-dimer off anticoagulation, residual thrombus, and proximal DVT or PE. Annual review of bleeding risk is essential.
key_points: Unprovoked PE with low bleeding risk warrants indefinite anticoagulation
Recurrence rate after stopping anticoagulation for unprovoked VTE is approximately 10% at 1 year
Elevated D-dimer off anticoagulation predicts higher recurrence risk
Reduced-dose rivaroxaban (10 mg) or apixaban (2.5 mg BD) can be used for extended prevention
Annual bleeding risk reassessment determines whether to continue or stop
explanation.pearls: Male sex is an independent risk factor for VTE recurrence after unprovoked events - the HERDOO2 rule identifies low-risk women who may safely stop anticoagulation, but no equivalent low-risk group exists for men.

stem: A 22-year-old tall, thin man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea that began 3 hours ago while at rest. He is a non-smoker with no significant medical history. He is not distressed and is speaking in full sentences. A chest X-ray confirms a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with a visible lung edge approximately 3 cm from the chest wall at the hilum.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 122/78 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 97% RA, Temp 36.8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Observe for 4 hours and repeat chest X-ray before discharge
options.rationale: Observation alone is appropriate for small primary spontaneous pneumothorax (less than 2 cm from the chest wall or less than 1 cm at the apex). This pneumothorax is moderate (3 cm from the chest wall at the hilum) and the patient is symptomatic, warranting active intervention with needle aspiration as the first step. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

options.text: Perform needle aspiration of the right hemithorax and reassess
options.rationale: For a symptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax larger than 2 cm, needle aspiration is the recommended first-line intervention. A 16-18G cannula is inserted in the second intercostal space, midclavicular line (or safe triangle) with aspiration of air using a syringe and three-way tap. If successful (lung re-expands on repeat X-ray), the patient can be discharged with outpatient follow-up. If aspiration fails, an intercostal catheter is the next step. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

options.text: Insert a large-bore intercostal drain connected to underwater seal
options.rationale: A large-bore intercostal drain (chest tube) is not first-line for primary spontaneous pneumothorax. It is more invasive, more painful, and associated with longer hospital stays than needle aspiration. Current guidelines recommend aspiration as the first intervention, with chest drain insertion reserved for failed aspiration or secondary pneumothorax. Small-bore drains (10-14F) are preferred over large-bore when drainage is needed. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange urgent cardiothoracic surgical review for thoracoscopy
options.rationale: Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) with pleurodesis is reserved for recurrent pneumothorax, persistent air leak beyond 5-7 days, bilateral pneumothorax, or high-risk occupations. A first episode of primary spontaneous pneumothorax is managed conservatively with aspiration or drainage. Surgical referral at this stage is premature. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Administer high-flow oxygen at 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask
options.rationale: Supplemental oxygen can accelerate pneumothorax reabsorption (by approximately 4-fold) by reducing the partial pressure of nitrogen in the blood. However, high-flow oxygen alone is not the primary intervention for a moderate symptomatic pneumothorax. It can be used as an adjunct alongside aspiration but does not replace the need for active air removal. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax occurs in young, tall, thin individuals without underlying lung disease. Management depends on size and symptoms. Small asymptomatic pneumothorax can be observed. Symptomatic or moderate-to-large pneumothorax (>2 cm on chest X-ray) is managed with needle aspiration as first-line. Chest drain insertion follows failed aspiration. Recurrent pneumothorax or persistent air leak warrants surgical referral for VATS and pleurodesis.
key_points: Needle aspiration is first-line for symptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax >2 cm
Small (<2 cm) asymptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax can be observed
If aspiration fails (lung does not re-expand), insert a small-bore intercostal drain
Surgical referral (VATS pleurodesis) is for recurrent episodes, persistent air leak, or bilateral pneumothorax
Supplemental oxygen accelerates reabsorption but does not replace active intervention for larger pneumothoraces
explanation.pearls: Needle aspiration for primary spontaneous pneumothorax has an immediate success rate of approximately 60-80% - if the first attempt fails (>2.5 L aspirated without resistance or lung fails to re-expand), a second attempt is not recommended; proceed to intercostal drain.

stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of congestive heart failure and type 2 diabetes presents with progressive dyspnoea over 2 weeks. He has right-sided reduced breath sounds with dullness to percussion. A chest X-ray confirms a large right-sided pleural effusion. A diagnostic thoracentesis is performed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 135/82 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 93% RA, Temp 36.9
data_table.Investigations: Serum: protein 62 g/L, LDH 180 U/L; Pleural fluid: protein 22 g/L, LDH 85 U/L, pH 7.42, glucose 5.8 mmol/L, cell count shows predominantly lymphocytes, no organisms on Gram stain

lead_in: How should this effusion be classified using Light's criteria?

options.text: Exudative, as the protein ratio exceeds the 0.5 threshold
options.rationale: The pleural fluid to serum protein ratio is 22/62 = 0.35, which is below the 0.5 threshold for an exudate. All three Light's criteria must be checked, and at least one must be met to classify an effusion as exudative. This ratio does not meet the exudative criterion. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Pleural Effusion)

options.text: Exudative, as the LDH ratio exceeds the 0.6 threshold value
options.rationale: The pleural fluid to serum LDH ratio is 85/180 = 0.47, which is below the 0.6 threshold for an exudate. This criterion is not met. An exudate requires at least one of three criteria to be positive: protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, or pleural fluid LDH above two-thirds the upper limit of serum normal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Transudative, as none of the three Light's criteria are met
options.rationale: Light's criteria classify an effusion as exudative if ANY one of three criteria is met: (1) pleural fluid protein/serum protein >0.5 (22/62 = 0.35 - not met), (2) pleural fluid LDH/serum LDH >0.6 (85/180 = 0.47 - not met), (3) pleural fluid LDH > two-thirds upper limit of normal serum LDH (if ULN = 250, threshold = 167; 85 < 167 - not met). As none are met, this is a transudate, consistent with heart failure. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Exudative, because the glucose level is below serum glucose
options.rationale: Glucose levels in pleural fluid can help identify complicated effusions (parapneumonic, empyema, rheumatoid, malignancy) when markedly low (<3.3 mmol/L), but glucose is not part of Light's criteria for classification. The glucose here is 5.8 mmol/L which is normal and does not indicate an exudate or a complicated effusion. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Pleural Effusion)

options.text: Indeterminate, requiring pleural biopsy for definitive typing
options.rationale: Light's criteria provide definitive classification in the vast majority of cases. 'Indeterminate' is not a Light's criteria category. The criteria are binary: if at least one criterion is met, the effusion is exudative; if none are met, it is transudative. Pleural biopsy is reserved for undiagnosed exudative effusions, particularly when malignancy or tuberculosis is suspected. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Light's criteria distinguish transudative from exudative pleural effusions using three biochemical parameters. An effusion is exudative if any one criterion is met: pleural/serum protein ratio >0.5, pleural/serum LDH ratio >0.6, or pleural LDH > two-thirds the upper limit of normal serum LDH. Transudative effusions are caused by systemic factors (heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome), while exudative effusions result from local processes (infection, malignancy, inflammation). Light's criteria have 98% sensitivity for exudates but may misclassify some transudates in diuretic-treated heart failure patients.
key_points: Light's criteria: protein ratio >0.5 OR LDH ratio >0.6 OR pleural LDH > two-thirds ULN for serum LDH
Any ONE criterion positive = exudate; all three negative = transudate
Common transudative causes: heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome
Common exudative causes: infection, malignancy, PE, autoimmune disease
Diuretics can concentrate transudates, causing misclassification as exudates - check serum-pleural albumin gradient if suspected
explanation.pearls: Light's criteria misclassify approximately 25% of heart failure transudates as exudates in patients on diuretics - if clinical suspicion for transudate is high despite meeting exudative criteria, a serum-to-pleural albumin gradient above 12 g/L confirms a true transudate.

stem: A 28-year-old woman with a BMI of 38 presents with a 6-week history of daily headaches that are worse on waking, bending forward, and straining. She reports transient visual obscurations lasting seconds, occurring several times daily. She recently started the combined oral contraceptive pill. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema with no retinal haemorrhages. Visual acuity is 6/9 bilaterally. Visual field testing shows enlarged blind spots. An MRI brain with MR venography is normal.

data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: no mass lesion, no hydrocephalus, no venous sinus thrombosis; partial empty sella, flattened posterior globes, tortuous optic nerve sheaths

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Commence topiramate 25 mg daily and titrate upward gradually
options.rationale: Topiramate has weak carbonic anhydrase inhibiting activity and can promote weight loss, making it useful as an adjunct in IIH. However, it is not as effective as acetazolamide at reducing CSF pressure and is considered second-line. It may be added if acetazolamide is not tolerated or as combination therapy for refractory cases. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: IIH)

options.text: Arrange urgent optic nerve sheath fenestration for vision threat
options.rationale: Optic nerve sheath fenestration is a surgical intervention reserved for rapidly progressive visual loss not responding to medical therapy or when medical therapy cannot be tolerated. This patient has mildly reduced acuity (6/9) and enlarged blind spots without severe visual field loss. Medical management should be trialled first. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: IIH)

options.text: Perform serial lumbar punctures weekly for therapeutic drainage
options.rationale: While therapeutic lumbar puncture can provide temporary symptom relief by reducing CSF pressure, the CSF is rapidly regenerated and serial LPs are not a sustainable management strategy. They are uncomfortable, carry procedural risks (infection, CSF leak), and do not address the underlying pathophysiology. Medical therapy with acetazolamide is the appropriate long-term approach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Commence acetazolamide 250 mg BD, cease the OCP, and advise weight loss
options.rationale: The initial management of IIH involves acetazolamide (a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces CSF production), cessation of exacerbating medications (combined oral contraceptive pill increases thrombotic risk and may worsen IIH), and weight loss (a 5-10% weight reduction can result in resolution). The IIHTT trial confirmed acetazolamide plus weight loss improves visual outcomes. Regular visual field monitoring is essential. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Prescribe sumatriptan as needed for the headaches and review monthly
options.rationale: The headaches are caused by raised intracranial pressure, not migraine. Treating with sumatriptan would address only the symptom without treating the underlying cause, risking progressive visual loss. The papilloedema and visual obscurations indicate raised ICP that requires CSF pressure-lowering therapy, not simple analgesia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension is characterised by raised intracranial pressure without a structural cause, most commonly affecting young obese women. The modified Dandy criteria require papilloedema, normal neuroimaging (may show MRI signs of raised ICP), elevated opening pressure on LP (>25 cmH2O), and normal CSF composition. Treatment involves acetazolamide, weight loss, and cessation of exacerbating medications. Surgical options (VP shunt, optic nerve sheath fenestration, venous sinus stenting) are reserved for refractory or vision-threatening cases.
key_points: IIH management triad: acetazolamide, weight loss (5-10% body weight), and cessation of exacerbating medications
Combined oral contraceptive pill should be ceased and switched to a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method
Visual field testing is the critical monitoring parameter - acuity may be preserved until late
LP opening pressure >25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition confirms the diagnosis
MRI signs include partial empty sella, flattened posterior globe, tortuous optic nerve sheaths
explanation.pearls: Transient visual obscurations (brief seconds-long episodes of visual blurring or greying out) are caused by transient ischaemia of the swollen optic nerve head and are a warning sign of visual compromise - their frequency correlates with the risk of permanent visual loss.

stem: A 55-year-old man is referred to the sleep clinic by his GP for assessment of excessive daytime sleepiness and witnessed apnoeas. His wife reports loud snoring with episodes of gasping and choking during sleep. He has a BMI of 34, neck circumference of 44 cm, and a Mallampati score of IV. He has resistant hypertension requiring three antihypertensive medications. His Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 16 out of 24.

data_table.Examination: Crowded oropharynx, large tongue, retrognathia, neck circumference 44 cm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Overnight pulse oximetry at home as a screening assessment
options.rationale: Overnight pulse oximetry can detect cyclical desaturation patterns suggestive of OSA but has limited sensitivity (approximately 60-70%) and cannot quantify the apnoea-hypopnoea index or distinguish obstructive from central events. Given the high pre-test probability in this patient, a more definitive investigation is warranted. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association)

options.text: Lateral cephalometric X-ray to assess upper airway anatomy
options.rationale: Cephalometric X-ray can identify anatomical contributors to OSA (retrognathia, macroglossia, reduced posterior airway space) but is not a diagnostic test for OSA. It may be useful pre-operatively if maxillomandibular advancement surgery is being considered, but the priority is confirming the diagnosis and quantifying severity with a sleep study. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association)

options.text: CT neck to exclude structural upper airway obstruction first
options.rationale: CT of the neck is not part of the routine diagnostic pathway for OSA. The clinical history and examination already identify significant risk factors (obesity, large neck, crowded oropharynx, retrognathia). Structural imaging is not needed before proceeding to a sleep study unless a specific structural lesion (tumour, goitre) is suspected. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association)

options.text: Arterial blood gas to assess for chronic respiratory failure
options.rationale: ABG can identify chronic hypercapnia (obesity hypoventilation syndrome) which may coexist with OSA. However, it does not diagnose OSA itself and should not delay the sleep study. ABG is indicated if there is clinical suspicion of obesity hypoventilation (daytime hypercapnia, polycythaemia) but is not the first investigation for OSA. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association)

options.text: Polysomnography to confirm diagnosis and determine severity
options.rationale: Polysomnography (in-laboratory or home-based level 2-3 study) is the gold-standard investigation for diagnosing and grading OSA severity. It measures the apnoea-hypopnoea index (AHI), which guides management: mild (5-15), moderate (15-30), severe (>30). This patient has a high clinical probability of moderate-to-severe OSA (ESS 16, witnessed apnoeas, resistant hypertension, obesity, large neck), and the AHI will determine CPAP eligibility. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association)

explanation.summary: Obstructive sleep apnoea is diagnosed by polysomnography, which measures the apnoea-hypopnoea index. OSA severity is graded by AHI: mild (5-15), moderate (15-30), severe (>30). Risk factors include obesity, large neck circumference (>40 cm women, >43 cm men), crowded oropharynx, retrognathia, and male sex. OSA is an important cause of resistant hypertension. CPAP is the first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe OSA and can improve blood pressure control, daytime sleepiness, and cardiovascular outcomes.
key_points: Polysomnography is the gold-standard diagnostic investigation for OSA
AHI severity grading: mild 5-15, moderate 15-30, severe >30 events per hour
CPAP is first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe OSA
OSA is present in up to 80% of patients with resistant hypertension
Epworth Sleepiness Scale >10 suggests excessive daytime sleepiness warranting investigation
explanation.pearls: Resistant hypertension (uncontrolled BP despite three antihypertensives including a diuretic) should trigger screening for OSA - treating the OSA with CPAP can reduce systolic blood pressure by 5-10 mmHg and may allow medication reduction.
